Makepeace Business System
Module 5 • Pitching the Deal
Transcript 	Page  of 28
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
I want to welcome everybody. We have a good group today.
Writing proposals is kind of an interesting proposition. For those of us who are writers or
marketers, we tend to do a lot of writing to prospects, and prospects tend to be kind of a
gray area. We’re all used to writing to prospects that we don’t see. We don’t know them.
We have samples of other writers’ work that we can emulate or at least learn from.
So that’s one thing, but then when it comes to writing a proposal a lot of us tend to freeze.
We think, “Oh, my goodness, this is a whole different animal. I have to really kind of
overdo this thing and go into unknown, kind of forbidden, territory with it,” and the opposite
is actually true.
I’ve been on the receiving end of many, many proposals, as well as having written many
for my clients over the years. The fact of the matter is that pretty much every dollar I’ve
earned since the early ‘90s can be traced to some kind of proposal that I made to a client.
In a very real way, writing these proposals is the most important sales copy you will ever
write, because they structure the deal that then allows you to go on and make tons of
money with a particular client, if your proposal and the resulting contract is favorable to
both of you.
Having said all that, one of the things I’ve been thinking about is that the best proposal is
the one you never have to write. It’s the one that’s just basically a verbal thing: “Hey, why
don’t I do this and you pay me that,” and you’re working with a client that you respect and
trust, with whom you have a history. He says, “Yes” and you go to work and you collect the
money. Simple, right?
The second best proposal is one that you can write on the back of a cocktail napkin. Some
of the greatest breakthroughs in history have been written on the back of a cocktail napkin.
For example, Art Laffer, who originated the Laffer Curve, one of the key points of supply
side economics, demonstrated it on the back of a cocktail napkin. In fact, he did it several
times and now those napkins go for fairly good prices among people who appreciate
economics. Simplicity is the key to a great proposal. And beyond that, it should be part of a
natural process.
Several years ago, I had been working freelance for Weiss Research. I wrote a promotion
for Safe Money Report, and then I wrote a promotion for another newsletter they had at
the time called the Y2K Alert, and they were gangbuster promotions. They brought in tons
of new subscribers at break-even or better, and for the first time in a long time, Martin’s list
was beginning to grow.
But more importantly, during that process Martin and I had become buddies. We talked

-- 1 of 28 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 5 • Pitching the Deal
Transcript 	Page 2 of 28
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
about this before, that one of the most important components of any partnership is
chemistry between you and the business owner you’re working with. And Martin and I had
it.
We’re both very difficult people. We’re both very quirky. Martin is very left brain, detail
oriented, and tends to begin approaching every idea from a negative viewpoint, so he
shoots holes in them. I tend to be more right brain, and I tend to be more optimistic. So I
tend to see every idea as a great idea.
I’m never a 100% right and he’s never 100% right. So we balance each other, and that
chemistry has worked well for us, and learning to appreciate that and really love that in
each other has really been the linchpin of our relationship.
I’m not just throwing that out there. It’s a key in choosing the clients you’re going to work
for, that not only have you proven, or you feel that you could create winners for him and he
agrees, but also that you enjoy working together and you trust each other.
So anyway, we took our wives on a Caribbean cruise together, a little 5-day cruise just to
relax. We thought it might be a fun time. We took our two young children with us. I was still
working for his competitors. I was still doing a lot of work in the health field as well as the
financial field, and I had no agenda.
My goal in life there was to go hang out, eat some good food, see some sights, have a
good time. But I could tell that Martin wasn’t relaxed. He did have an agenda. He just kept
saying things that made it clear to me that he was kind of poking around, trying to figure
out a way to deepen the work relationship, and get more out of me.
We’re both real early risers, so we both get up around three or four in the morning. Finally,
one morning about two or three mornings into the cruise, we’re both sitting up on deck,
just chatting, and he made a couple of comments about how we’d made such a good start
and that there was so much potential.
I just looked at him and I said, “Do you want to take this thing to the moon? Do you want to
just focus all our energies on it and ride this horse until it won’t ride anymore?” And he got
all excited and said, “Absolutely!” So the next couple of days we spent a couple of hours
every day just kind of chatting about what was needed in his company.
First, what were the opportunities, and second, what his challenges were and why he
wasn’t taking advantage of those opportunities. We talked about the strengths and
weaknesses in his company in terms of personnel and infrastructure. We talked about his
management style and how it was, in some cases, really helping the company, but also in
some cases it was holding us back. W talked basically about the whole scope of it.

-- 2 of 28 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 5 • Pitching the Deal
Transcript 	Page  of 28
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
By the time we were done there, I was able to go back, crank out a one page letter and
say, “Here’s what we’re going to do. Here’s what you’re going to pay me.” And within a
couple of weeks we had a contract. Within a month I was drawing very nice royalty checks
from Martin.
You’re not going to be able to emulate that kind of situation in every case, but if you take
that as the ideal and think about what happened there, I think it’s going to help you in
writing your proposals.
One of the major errors people make is jumping into writing the proposal too soon. You
may say to a client, “You need a new direct mail piece to promote this,” or “We need
to form a partnership,” or whatever the idea is, and the client says, “Alright, give me a
proposal.”
Well, what do you do now? How far do you go with this proposal? Do you have to sell him
on the idea of working together? Do you have to sell him on every idea you could possibly
come up with? And how are you going to do that before you really know what his vision is,
what his company strengths and weaknesses are and all the other things?
The proposal itself should really be kind of in the middle, almost the tipping point of the
process that takes you from the handshake or the idea of the deeper relationship, all the
way through to the money on the other side.
The process really is that first you need to talk with the decision maker. And first of all, you
need to know what his vision is, what his frustrations are, what his fears are, what he’s
eager to get done. What would he love to have someone else do that he doesn’t have to
mess with? And some other things. We’ll look at some of the questions that need to be
asked in that all-important interview.
Secondly, and this is something I haven’t included in what we’re going to do today, but I
think it’s really important to visit the company and to learn from the staff.
The fact of the matter is, and I run a company so I know this is true - the person at the top
only thinks he knows what’s really going on. He has a view of how his staff interacts, how
things happen, who’s competent and who isn’t, and it’s partial at best, certainly when it
comes to the problems.
So get involved with the staff. Take a day and ask to meet all of his key managers and
spend a little bit of time with each one of them, and find out what their vision is and
whether it’s consistent with the owner’s vision. What problems do they see? What are their
frustrations?
You’re essentially asking them, “What can I do for you? What is it that you want?”

-- 3 of 28 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 5 • Pitching the Deal
Transcript 	Page  of 28
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Then the third step is to go to the web and research his competitors and also get a
full understanding of what your target company is actually doing. That’s an excellent
opportunity to pick up new ideas that you may or may not otherwise have come up with.
At this point, now you know where the decision maker is. You know where the staff is. You
know where the competition is. And you have your own set of ideas about what you want
to do with this company. Now it’s time to write the proposal.
Just as a minor point – maybe not so minor – the word “proposal” is a little bit intimidating,
or it can be, especially to underlings at a company with whom you want to work. I like
to call my proposals “discussion documents.” By calling it a discussion document, I’m
making it clear that this is not what I’m going to do, that these are my ideas and I want the
employees’ input.
Now the staff feels like they’re part of the team. They’re not being shunted aside by this
expert from out of town with a briefcase. They’re much less likely to be intimidated. That
means, by the way, that they’re much less likely to be nailing you to a cross every time you
leave the room, which is one of the things that happen quite often when you’re an outsider
working with the company.
So then, from that proposal or discussion document, the next step, which is made clear
from the outset, is a discussion to revise and finalize the proposal – which in essence is a
plan of where you’re going to go from now.
Then the proposal should set up the contract and make negotiating the contract a no-
brainer. Then the next step is that you start cashing checks from the work you’ve done.
Let’s take a look at some off the steps that I think about when I’m creating a proposal or a
discussion document, and how I kind of move through the process in a linear fashion.
It’s important to understand that not all of these steps will apply. I think it was Cheryl who
wrote to me and said that she is in the middle of preparing a proposal right now. It’s a
different kind of proposal. She’s actually been retained by her client to write a proposal for
the client to the government, and they’re not selling a product, they’re selling services.
So some of the broader principles that we’re going to cover will, I think, come in very
handy for you. However, you are going to have to think your way through how that kind of
proposal differs from the partnering proposals that we’re actually talking about today.
One of the phrases that I use most often around here is, “Ready! Fire! Aim!” So often we’re
in such a hurry to get things done that we don’t really stand back from a project and say,
“Hey, wait. What is our ultimate objective here? What effect do we want this document to

-- 4 of 28 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 5 • Pitching the Deal
Transcript 	Page  of 28
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
have on the person who is going to read it?”
Before you begin writing any proposal, it’s important to understand what your aims are.
When I’m creating a proposal, for example, the first thing I want to do is to confirm that my
mission is to fulfill my client’s fondest business desires.
I want to address the emotions that he has right now about his business, that he is either
enjoying the fantasy of explosive growth or what would he rather get rid of – like his
frustrations and his fears. That’s part of this initial interview with the client.
We also talked about going to the company and asking the same questions of the
managers so that they’re more likely to be on your side as they work through your finished
proposal.
Then you want to add something new, for several reasons. You want to come in with new
ideas, things they haven’t thought of, or better ways to do things that they are presently
contemplating.
We were approached a few weeks ago by a company that’s developing a software
product. They had been working on it for some time and they had some very clear ideas
of how they wanted this product to go, what they wanted it to do, how much they would
charge for it and how they would market it. But they contacted us because they wanted
help. They wanted more ideas.
We took a look at the product, and we said, “It’s a great product! But there are four or five
things about the way that you’re planning to develop the product that we think could be
done better. We think your pricing is absolutely wrong.”
And we were able to go through those things with this client. That is the proposal I’ll show
you with any parts that need to be redacted as soon as it’s complete.
By bringing those new things in, we made the pipe bigger. We were able to turn the one
product into three products. We were able to turn a $5 product into a $3,500 product. We
were able to give them ideas for positioning in the product that would make it palatable to
a far larger market segment than they were originally contemplating being able to access.
So you want to add something new. The document then, by doing that, provides value, a
reason why it should be read and thought about, but it also demonstrates your expertise
and you do those things.
However, you can imagine if you add something new without really understanding the
current situation in the corporation, what the division is, what the problems are, you are
likely to end up sitting there with your face hanging out while they tell you “Hey, we can’t

-- 5 of 28 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 5 • Pitching the Deal
Transcript 	Page  of 28
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
do that. We’ve done that before and it was a miserable failure. Our competitor did it and
he went out of business.” Or, “That’s not the direction we want to go,” or whatever their
objection is.
So by doing the interviews first, you’ll know what the objections are, so even if you want
to insist on an idea that you believe is right for the company, you will at least be able to
answer those objections in your proposal.
Finally, you want your proposal to include all the information an attorney would need
to begin drawing up your contract. Ideally, you want this to be a slippery slope for the
client. You want him to read the thing, love the ideas, get excited about your vision, be
comfortable with your compensation package and be able to just email your proposal to
his attorney and say, “Draw up a contract.”
I think we’ll mention as we get further into this; I’ll say it now and then I’ll say it again later
– one of the mistakes I made early on in my career was that I was timid about presenting
my compensation up front in the proposal. That is a serious error. If you do everything
else right, the client is counting the money and the benefit of having you aboard as you’re
presenting your ideas.
For the first part of the thing, he’s going to be just thrilled. He’s going to be excited. But you
see, if you get down to the point of saying, “You’re going to have to pay me every month
and you’re going to have to pay me this much in sales and these royalties and all this
other stuff” – and he freezes, then you know right away that you have a problem. And you
can either negotiate it or you can walk away and save a lot of time.
If your compensation isn’t in the initial proposal, and you say, “Well, let’s just talk about
what we want to do together,” because you want to avoid conflict, you’re just going to wind
up wasting time with a lot of the prospects you deal with. So make sure this is a complete
document, something that you can just simply hand to an attorney and say, “Draw up a
contract.”
Let’s look at some of the questions that I like to ask business owners during the
exploratory phase prior to beginning my proposal writing.
I want to know what his mission statement is. This is not some kind of phony baloney,
bullshit business school thing. This is, “Where do you want to take your company? Why
are you in this business?”
You want to hear something like, “Well, you know, we want to bring value to people’s lives.
We want to do it by making their lawn greener, or making their waist thinner or putting
more hair on their head,” or whatever it is that the company does. You want to hear that
the owner has a passion for that.

-- 6 of 28 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 5 • Pitching the Deal
Transcript 	Page  of 28
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
You can draw him out and get stories about why he is passionate about that. This kind of
thing is going to give you a very clear vision what your client is ultimately going to ask you
to do, and the full spectrum of the kind of work you’ll be doing for them.
Then I ask, “What’s your short, medium and long-term vision for your company?” Now, this
mission statement has more to do with what impact you want to have on the world. Martin
Weiss wants to help people protect their money in bad times and help them make money
in good times. Health Resources wants to help people live better and healthier, without
having to pay a fortune for doctors or drugs. Every company is driven by some passionate
central desire.
The next one, the vision for the company, is “How do you see the company growing? How
would you like to see it growing? How quickly would you like to see it grow? How high? Do
you want to create your own special niche? Do you want to be #1 in the existing niche or
several niches? Which niches are those?”
And when you’re talking to the owner especially, you want to know what he sees as his
involvement, going forward. One of the things I think of at the very beginning, with every
new client, is, “How is this going to end?”
I remember Layne Lowery, when we first started talking at Health Resources, I asked him,
“What’s your vision? Do you want to run this company for the rest of your life? You’re a
young man, that could happen. You could run this company for another 30 years. Or do
you want to build it up to $100 million dollars in a couple of years and then sell it and go on
to do something else? What is it that you want to do? What challenges do you think have
to be overcome to achieve your vision?”
This is where the business owner, and in turn the different managers, get to bitch. They
get to tell you, “Oh man, we’ve been trying to do this for so long.” Or, “I can’t find good
employees.” Or, “I can’t find good copy” Or, “I can’t find good product developers.”
Whatever the challenges are that have kept him from achieving his vision so far.
What are his greatest frustrations in terms of achieving the vision? This is another way of
asking this. But you want to dig a little deeper, because ultimately, guys, he’s not going to
sign your contract because it’s logical to do so. He’s going to tell himself that, and it needs
to be logical to do so, but ultimately what’s going to get you the deal you’re looking for is
making the business owner see that you will be able to fulfill his most powerful, positive
desires and emotions, and ease or get rid of the negative ones.
Dig in deep there. What’s your greatest fear? We all have them. A business owner has to
make payroll every single month. Even if he has more money than God, his cash reserves
in that company he watches like a hawk, and any time he goes through a month or a
quarter where those start receding, that’s a huge fear for him.

-- 7 of 28 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 5 • Pitching the Deal
Transcript 	Page 8 of 28
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Maybe his fear is a regulatory one. Maybe it’s a fear of litigation, that a customer might
sue him. Find out what his greatest fear is, because that will be a key to a great working
relationship, and also to a great proposal.
Ask him why he thinks people buy his product. Sometimes I get some very funny answers
to that question, and sometimes they’re very instructive. I’ve told the story before of how
one company I work with, they sell a three dimensional product. But their customer service
people are always available if you have a question or you don’t like the product and you
want to return it – whatever the thing is, or if you want to order more. There’s a toll-free
number and people are encouraged to buy it.
Well, his market is older. His customer service manager came to him and said, “Look, I just
started at your company. I’m from another company and we limited our customer service
calls to three minutes each. Your customer service calls are an average of 15 minutes
each. We need to let people know that when they call in, they can’t be telling us about their
aches and pains and their daughter who just graduated college and all this other stuff.
They need to get their business done and get off the phone.”
My client said, “Hey, you’re nuts. What we’re going to do is hire more customer service
people. Because our people are older, they’re bored. It’s a high point in their day to be able
to call us and talk to someone who’s friendly and who’s interested in them. That’s part of
our product. So we’re going to add that to it.” So you’ll get some interesting answers there.
Dig in.
What are the most common causes for customer complaints? This question helps you on
two levels. Number one, and I’ll tell you what the owner and the managers think the most
common cause is, but hopefully you’ll be able to dig deeper and find out what it actually is.
The two are quite different, quite often.
Another thing that you need to look at is what the frequency of customer complaints is.
You’d be surprised at how many companies are being run on the basis of some underling
walking into the boss’ office and saying, “We’re getting a ton of complaints about that
promotion we sent out yesterday.”
And the boss goes, “Oh no, that’s terrible, a ton of complaints! Let’s change the
promotion,” or “Let’s change the strategy,” or “Let’s do this…”
You’d be shocked to know how many business owners say, “Out of our total list,
what percent is that? Out of the total people who received that promotion, how many
complained?” Or, “Out of the total number of people who bought that product, how many
complained?”
Without putting it in perspective, that statement from the person who’s coming to the

-- 8 of 28 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 5 • Pitching the Deal
Transcript 	Page  of 28
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
owner with a problem is meaningless, and certainly shouldn’t be acted upon. But most
business owners don’t see it as that. They don’t have time to do the analysis, so they take
that kind of baloney at face value, and in most cases it costs them money.
So you want to know what the owner thinks the most common causes of complaints
are, but you also want to know what the most common causes really are and what
percentage, and how high his complaint volume is on each of the top topics that people
are complaining about.
See, you thought this was going to be all about sitting around and typing up a proposal.
Like everything else in life, the actual writing of the proposal is less important than the
thinking behind it.
You need to also ask the owner who his strongest managers are, what the weakest
areas are on his staff, what he’s tried that didn’t work and why he thinks it failed. This can
apply to marketing, but it can also apply to product development, corporate structure,
promoting particular people to particular positions. Ask them “What do you think has been
your biggest failure in running this company over the last couple of years?” It’s great
intelligence to have and it will give you a lot of ideas that you can then pass on for helping
the company grow.
These are the two critical questions: What do you want from me? You don’t have to ask
it that way, but everything that you’ve asked so far has been leading up to this: What is it
that I can do for you?
“Well, I want you to build my list. I have 100,000 customers; I want 200,000 customers.”
“I’m pulling in $100 a month from my average customer on the back end; I want to pull in
$200 a month from my average customer on the back end.”
Or he may just say, “I want to double sales,” or, “I want to cut my costs,” or, “I want to beat
the living daylights out of any competitor.”
What do you want from me? Tell me what you want me to do.
Then, what are you willing to give me if I give you what you want? Again, this is not how
you ask the question, but before you waste time writing a big proposal, I like to give some
clear cues as to what this is going to cost them.
One company that we’re negotiating with right now, I told them right up front that if I’m
writing a customer acquisition program for the mail, you pay me $50 per $1,000. If I’m
writing one for the web, you pay me $5-$6 per lead. If I’m writing a promotion to sell
something to the house file, you’re going to pay me 10-20% on the sales.

-- 9 of 28 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 5 • Pitching the Deal
Transcript 	Page 0 of 28
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
If I’m getting involved with the organization or company, with staffing, procedures or with
the other things that really turn a Model T company into Bugatti marketing machine, then
I want 10-20% of your stock. If the client looks at me and smiles and says, “Not a single
problem there!” then I know that the deal is basically done. I just have some paperwork to
do.
If he blanches, then I want to know right up front what his objections are and what his
concerns are. I was talking to a guy about two months ago, and he had two concerns.
One was litigation, the other one was cash management. He’s contemplating turning the
marketing over to us, period. Those were his concerns.
He was happy with the money I was thinking about. We’ve worked together before and
he knew what I could provide. But he had those two concerns and knowing that early on
really helped me with the proposal.
We’re getting some questions. Let me address them.
Cheryl says –
You have a history with Martin, but what about new clients? Are there triggering events,
things going on in the news or economy or specific industry niches, that you would
recommend that we pay attention to or look for?
Good question, Cheryl. A couple of things. First of all, yes, I had a history with Martin, but a
big part of what I’ve been saying so far is that you need to develop some kind of a history
with your prospective client prior to spending the time and effort of trying to put together a
long term relationship.
That experience can be as simple as I just mentioned – spending time with him or his
company, and really getting to know them and making sure that you make plenty of time
for social time, so that you can get to know each other on a social level as well as in the
office, because first of all, there’s bonding there that will make things easier, and second,
you’ll actually learn a lot.
I’ve learned more about Martin’s company at sushi bars than I have in his office. So the
relationship is important, and you can build a history, even if it’s not a work history.
There are situations where we’re approached because of our reputation. People have
heard of us, so they already know what we can provide and everything, and they want to
work with us, so we don’t have a working relationship to start with.
There are other times where you may approach a company that hasn’t heard of you.
Then you have to begin building a relationship from scratch and you do that through

-- 10 of 28 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 5 • Pitching the Deal
Transcript 	Page  of 28
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
the interview process, through visiting the company, and through making sure that the
business owner and the company have a really good view of what you bring to the table,
what you’ve done before for other people.
That’s the first part of your question. The second part of your question –
If you’re contemplating setting up a partnership agreement with a particular company in a
given market or niche, by all means you need to immerse yourself in everything about that
niche.
When I say everything, I mean subscribe to the industry publications, watch the news, and
search the news online. You can go to Yahoo! News and search for any news about pretty
much any sector of the economy. Find out what’s going on in that sector. Is it experiencing
problems? Is it expanding? Is it contracting? How are oil prices affecting it? How are other
rising commodity prices affecting it?
When you sit down and talk to this company about where they’re going, they don’t want
to talk to someone who doesn’t know their industry from a hole in the ground. I see this a
lot in the investment field. Guys, if any of you want to write for investment companies, do
what Harun is doing right now. Harun is one of my copywriters here at Response, Ink.
On his own initiative - no one suggested that he do this – he gets up at 7:00 (this morning
it was at 7:12, I hear) in order to attend a 7:15 conference call with Weiss Research and I.
On that call we talk about what happened in the economy yesterday, what’s happening on
our list, what’s happening with our business.
So Harun is getting an earful of what the Fed’s lack of action yesterday really means, what
rising oil prices mean, what the softening demand in the U.S. for energy means. He’s gone
to school on the economy and on the investment markets, and he’s doing everything he
can to come up to speed.
Now, when Harun leaves me, like Newtson just did, and goes out and starts his own
consulting practice or his own freelance practice or begins partnering with companies, and
he has a financial client and the financial client says, “Well, you know, we’re really worried
about CDOs or derivatives” or some exotic options, Harun will know exactly what they’re
talking about.
When the client says something like “The Fed is loaning hundreds of billions of dollars at
the discount window,” Harun will know that’s inflationary and he’ll know why that’s a bad
thing.
Know the industry. There’s so many times when Martin, and my other financial clients, has
hired great writers who just don’t understand the investment industry and the results have

-- 11 of 28 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 5 • Pitching the Deal
Transcript 	Page 2 of 28
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
been pathetic.
Good question, Cheryl, and it’s a big part of preparing.
Here’s Larry Mekus. Hi, Larry. He asks –
If someone pays me X dollars per lead, how do you verify how many leads he has coming
in?
Well, it’s very simple. Whether you do the leads on your own system, where people opt-in
– for example, let’s say it’s an Internet campaign, which most people are doing these days.
If you’re doing it on your own system, you absolutely know how many are coming in. If the
people opt-in and the name goes directly to the company, then the company will know.
But as part of your working agreement with the company, you need to have access to their
system so that you can watch those leads as they come in.
Also, there’s another very important thing here. The whole thing of direct marketing is
monitoring results. “I spent $10,000 on pay per click this month; how many leads did I
get, and what was my cost per lead?” So you can’t do a job for the client unless you have
those numbers.
I have a client now that’s spending $350,000 per month on pay per click.”Alright, fine. I
want to see how many leads are coming in on that and I want to see what his cost is per
lead, because we’re writing the pay per click ads and we’re writing the squeeze pages and
we’re writing the sales pages and the gauntlets that follow. For me to do my job, I have to
have those numbers. It would be foolish for the client to hold out on me in that case.
In direct mail, it’s a little more difficult if the lead is coming back in over the telephone or if
it’s coming back in through the mail.
The bottom line is, no piece of paper is worth anything, no contract is worth anything,
unless the people who sign it are trustworthy. If you suspect that you’re being ripped off or
if you suspect that a client is being untrustworthy, don’t even bother signing the contract
because his signature is worthless.
In my contracts I quite often will have auditing clauses that give me the right to audit the
client at any time. So if I see an anomaly, if it seems to me that the numbers I’m seeing
are suppressed or depressed in some way, I have the right to demand an audit. I can go
in and look at their source documents and the reports from their call center or the reports
from the mailroom.
I’ve never exercised that because I’ve never signed contracts with people I don’t trust.

-- 12 of 28 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 5 • Pitching the Deal
Transcript 	Page  of 28
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Now you’ve gathered all your information. You basically know what the client wants, what
the client needs, what he expects from you, and what he’s willing to pay you.
Now it’s a matter of sitting down and putting it on paper in a way that’s likely to give you a
quick acceptance. First of all, think of your proposal as B2B sales copy. That’s essentially
what it is, but it’s not B2B sales copy to a nameless, faceless prospect. You need to
respect your client’s time.
Remember what I said: the best proposal is one that you never write or that you can write
on the back of a napkin. The simpler and the quicker that you can communicate your
ideas, the better. The more you let your eyes roll up in your head and just kind of “blah,
blah, blah” and give him a more substantial document, the less likely he is to read it.
Respect your client’s intelligence, too. Avoid hyperbole. Avoid overstatement and
exaggeration. Just tell it straight. Just be straight with him. Be short, be straight. Don’t
forget that other people will read this.
He’s going to show this to everyone on his staff whose opinion he trusts or respects, or
everyone he feels he has to show it to, because he’s contemplating major expenditure with
you or a relationship that may change the inner workings of his company to some degree.
Other people will read this. If you’ve done this right, you’re sensitive to where the
kingdoms are within his empire. You know who the strong people are, who the paranoid
people are, who the insecure people are, who the real performers are.
Keep that in mind as you’re writing this. Do not include tactical things in proposals that will
make any of the key decision makers in the company the slightest bit paranoid.
Anticipate and answer questions and objections. You don’t even have to raise the question
or the objection. But if you know from talking to people, from doing your research, that
there are going to be questions, or “Oh boy, Fred’s going to object to this one,” then
load that up with the reasons you would give Fred, if he made that objection in person, if
possible without actually raising the objection.
Then provide some value, but not too much. Don’t feel like you have to lay out their
entire marketing plan for the whole year. Don’t feel like you have to teach them how to do
marketing your way, or show them how to do a break-even calculation or anything else.
Provide value in the form of quick suggestions, contacts, or tools.
For example, let’s say you’re writing copy and you know a great pay per click guy. One of
the things that you may mention in your proposal is, “I’ll put you in touch with my friend so
and so who does the pay per click work for IBM.”

-- 13 of 28 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 5 • Pitching the Deal
Transcript 	Page  of 28
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
The point of providing that sum value of the contract is readership, just like it is in sales
copy – readership and credibility. It demonstrates your expertise. But there’s a danger in
providing too much, because the more detail that you give people about what you’re going
to do, the more possible objections you’re going to get.
I was involved in a call yesterday that drove me nuts. I finally just hung up in frustration. I
have sent through five ideas to name an online event. There were five people on the call
and immediately they started parsing each one of the words in each one of these titles and
subtitles.
Now this group, if you gave them the title “Gone With The Wind” or “War and Peace” or
“The Holy Bible,” they would tear the living crap out of it and they would tell you all the
reasons why that was the worst title ever written, because of this word or that word, or this
nuance or that nuance. Frankly, if you left them in a room long enough with it, they’d end
up with something that was so horrible it couldn’t be used.
So I finally just said, “Look, there are too many cooks spoiling this chowder. I’m out of
here. Just pick one, I don’t care.” And that was the end of that. Now that’s what happens
when you provide a proposal that has way too much detail in it. You’re giving people little
hooks that the can grab onto and extend this process.
All the details can be worked out later. It’s the working agreement and the general direction
that you want to cover in your proposal. You want to sell the dream, right up front.
Now we’re beginning to talk about the actual structure of the proposal. At the very
beginning of the proposal, you want to sell the dream. You want to be specific about it.
This is where you regurgitate what the owner’s dream is, where he wants to take the
company, what he wants to do in terms of the company’s mission statement, all of the
things he told you he wants you to do for him in those interviews.
Then take the dream farther. You’re painting a painting. Give it some dimension. Think
about some of the other things. A guy may tell you his product is the best in the industry
but he doesn’t get any recognition. What he’s really saying is he wants to be president of
his trade group. He may be saying, “I need a little recognition here.”
If you think he’s saying that, then be thinking about it. Read between the lines and give
him what he wants here. Draw the picture of where his company will be in three years.
Then tell how you’re going to get there.
I always say when you’re writing copy, “Tell them what you’re going to tell them. Tell them.
Then tell them what you told them.” This is where you’re going to tell them what you’re
telling them. This is just big, broad strokes.

-- 14 of 28 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 5 • Pitching the Deal
Transcript 	Page  of 28
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
“What we’re going to do is create a marketing group. The marketing group is going to do
this, that and the other thing. We’re going to have campaigns in these media. Our goal is
to bring in this many new customers, new leads per month.”
You have other metrics like cost per sale or return on investment. This is the executive
summary. This is particularly important if your proposal runs more than four or five pages
long.
The next step is to listen to challenges. We just looked at where we want to go. Here are
the things that are holding us back. These are some of the things you want to look at: the
company, the spokesperson, and the product position.
One of the things you run into most commonly is the company is totally in love with the
product but they don’t have a spokesperson. People don’t bond with products, they bond
with people.
Let’s make the owner of the company a celebrity. Let’s make him the spokesperson. If he
doesn’t want to do that let’s go hire one. We have to put a face on this product.
How should the company be positioned? How should the product be positioned? I told you
the ESRC story so I won’t recount it. In that story you saw how simply repositioning the
product – not so much the company, but the product we were selling – rare coins, as not
something for numismatic nerds, but rather something that had a memory.
If this coin could talk what would it say? It was a connection to the romance of the Old
West. Just making that change in positioning was huge. If the company’s positioning could
be improved this is the place to talk about it.
Chances are, in many cases, the product line is fixed or at least static for the time being.
That’s fine. However, if there are ways to improve the product this is a good place to
talk about it. If you think the product is standing in the way of growth, if it needs to be
improved.
The infrastructure of the company. What do they need in terms of facilities to create the
change? What’s holding them back? Do they need a television studio? Do they need CD
duplicators? What hardware or software and other requirements do they need in order to
build a foundation for the growth you’re about to create in the company?
Part of your initial investigation of the company is you’re looking at how the company is
structured – how orders come down from the top and feedback comes back from the
bottom.
Unfortunately, the way corporations are structured is the customer is at the bottom end.

-- 15 of 28 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 5 • Pitching the Deal
Transcript 	Page  of 28
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
The marketing department is next. Then you have this whole bunch of lords and ladies
who are involved in bean counting, legal issues and all kinds of other personnel, etc., who
have never sold a thing in their lives actually running things.
The only input or feedback that matters, which is coming from customers, is filtering up
to the marketing people but they tend not to have a lot of authority in the company. That’s
one thing you’re really going to want to change when you move into a company.
You’re going to want to turn that thing upside down so that the customer is on top. The
marketing people will lead the charge because the customers are telling them what they
want. They’re getting the data.
The president, the bean counter, lawyer, personnel people, everyone else in the company,
their job is to help marketing do their job. You start your company that way and you’ve got
a winner on your hands.
Lead Production and Conversion -- If that’s something that is a challenge for them you’re
going to have to solve that problem for them. In part of the proposal you would simply
mention that fact that.
“Lead production is a problem. It has only produced this many leads and they’ve been
$10 a lead. What we need to do is develop online and offline lead production campaigns,
ones that would enable us to bring in ten times that number of leads at half the cost,” or
whatever your metrics are.
New Customer Acquisition -- This is a problem for every company. This is the big one.
You’re going to find they value this the most. New customer acquisition is the hardest thing
to do.
Sales to existing customers is the easiest thing, so some people make the mistake of
saying they’re going to put their best people on new customer acquisition because that’s
where they have their capital risk. It may cost $500 a thousand to put a promotion in the
mail. It may be $300,000 to produce an infomercial. We’ve got to have our best people
there and we’ll handle the house file ourselves.
To me that’s kind of wrong. You want the absolute best you can get in both places. That
means you as the consultant, copywriter, rainmaker, marketing guy needs to be involved
in both.
New customer acquisition is a big deal because it represents part of the growth equation.
Growth is the number of customers times sales. This is part of the equation. The other part
of it is how much can we sell to our existing customers, how much can we keep on board
with us?

-- 16 of 28 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 5 • Pitching the Deal
Transcript 	Page  of 28
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
You need to be involved in all three: customer acquisition, customer promotions, and
retention. Here’s where you outline the challenges in terms of customer acquisition.
“We’ve been able to find mailing lists that have performed better than a certain percent.
Our SEO traffic really sucks. Our pay per click is costing us too much. We budgeted a
million dollars for pay per click and we can’t spend more than $100,000 and still keep our
lead costs where it needs to be.” Whatever the problems are that you’ve encountered,
that’s where this goes.
Customer Service – One of the first things you do when you’re dealing with a company
is sit down all by yourself and figure out how to catch their customer service department
really screwing up. This is fun. I really enjoy doing this.
Figure out the nightmare scenario for a customer service person. Put them through their
paces. I think I told you that years ago, when I first started working with Weiss, I called up
their customer service people. I told them I just got a promotion for this save money report
thing and it looks kind of interesting. Tell me about what I get with it.
They haltingly told me about the premiums and how often I got the newsletter. Nothing
about what it would do for me, just the feature physical stuff. Then I asked how much it
was. They said it was $99 a year. They didn’t tell me that the price was $189 and this was
a discount.
Well, what if I wanted two years? Then they tell me if I take two years I get five more
reports and I get it for $189. I decide it sounds pretty good and tell them I’ll take three
years. They tell me I can’t get a three year subscription.
Quick email to the owner of the company saying, “You have five quick ways that you can
put money on the bottom line right now. Script your customer service people. People call
in asking questions about the product. Have them scripted to present the benefits of the
product, guarantee, the offer, and to never say we won’t take your order if you want three
years.”
It makes you a hero. It’s also a quick way to put money on the bottom. It’s also a way of
demonstrating value because you didn’t charge for that, you just did it.
At any rate, in your document here you’ll want to have done that research and have talked
to the customer service people. Address those challenges.
Retention – Make sure you’ve taken a look at the renewal rates or the reorder rates.
Outline the challenges they’re facing there.
Public Relations – A lot of people forget about this, especially people involved in direct

-- 17 of 28 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 5 • Pitching the Deal
Transcript 	Page 8 of 28
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
response marketing. I place high value on public relations. Go to Martin Weiss’ website
and look at all the media appearances his people are making around the world.
Larry Ellison, who is a real good buddy of mine, when he’s in Asia he hosts CNBC Asia
over there. That’s how good their public relations department is. Every time any one of
their guys publishes a book they’re all over the newspapers and TV. I’ve got to tell you, it
helps response.
You get more name recognition every time you get a full page ad on one of your clients in
USA Today, the Wall Street Journal or some place. You’re in the mail and you’re going to
see a spike in your response. So don’t forget the public relations possibilities.
Then outline your ideas for the product. Maybe it’s perfect but I doubt it. Look for
opportunities to improve the packaging, quality of the product, what it does, its
functionality, its durability and those kinds of things.
Look at the price and see if there are things that can be done there. Ask the questions.
You’re not a buyer’s rep but ask the questions. What if we bought it in Mexico or in China?
How are our competitors getting the best possible cost in each step of the process for
that?
Maybe we should test a higher price. Maybe we should test a lower price. Maybe we
should gang products together and increase our average sale. Look at his pricing and look
at his guarantees. Remember the principle, the longer the guarantee the fewer refunds
you’ll get.
Best guarantee I ever saw was on a package I wrote for Rodale. It was actually their idea.
It was a lifetime heirloom guarantee. “Buy this book and you or your heirs can return it any
time with no time limits and get your $29 back.” What does that do?
Contrast that with giving someone a two week guarantee. “Take the book, review it for two
weeks and if you don’t like it send it back and we’ll refund your money.” When you say two
weeks everybody has a calendar on their refrigerator. They put a big red X on that day two
weeks from now and right on the calendar, “Return Rodale book.” They know they have
that much time to read it and then they’ll return it.
If the deadline was off in the distance they’re going to be less likely to exercise the
guarantee. Take a look at that guarantee. Business owners will often go for the shorter
guarantee because it scares them to have a longer guarantee. It’s really a better way to
go.
You may run into some other issues with that which will become evident as you learn more
about the industry you’re working in and how they do their accounting. We’ll leave that for

-- 18 of 28 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 5 • Pitching the Deal
Transcript 	Page  of 28
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
another time.
Look at the premiums. What else do they get with the product? Let’s say you’re selling a
lawn mower, not an investment newsletter or health supplements or any of the stuff I deal
with. You’re selling a lawnmower. What can you add to the package that the customer gets
that brings additional value to him?
This is a great place and a lawnmower is a good example. Let’s say you’re Toro and
you’re lawnmowers. What does your customer really want? Does he want a lawnmower?
No, he doesn’t want a lawnmower. He wants a coupon for a free five gallon fill-up, or he
wants packets of flower seeds from another company that markets those.
Maybe he wants a free quart of oil for it. He might want a video or a book on how to have
the greenest lawn in the neighborhood, or how to make yard work go faster and easier.
Think about not just how can we junk this up with premiums, but how can we really bring
value to people’s lives and make that price seem like even more of a bargain?
New Product Development -- Outline your ideas for developing new products as well at
this point. However, I wouldn’t get into too much detail unless there is one or two of them
that you’re recommending they begin on immediately.
Also present your ideas for acquiring third party products. You’ve got a data base. If the
client has a product development problem and he can’t develop as many products as his
people would like to buy from him if he offered them the opportunity, then acquiring third
party products as a way to go.
We’ve done that and the cool thing here is it doesn’t always have to be the same kind of
product the client normally sells. I’ve sold water filters, water distillers, to investors. A friend
of mine, Jack, I met years ago through attending my power marketing summit imports and
markets the Water Wise Water Distiller. It’s the best water distiller in the world.
We introduced that to our file of then about 150,000-200,000 investors. They sold like
hotcakes. Don’t think about your clients’ customers monolithically. Just because they
bought a lawnmower doesn’t mean that’s the only thing they buy. They’re buying all kinds
of stuff.
Sure, if you stay in the niche where you have credibility you’re going to do very well with it.
You need to test products from other niches as well. The guy has a lawn so what does that
tell you? He’s probably a home owner, right? He’s got a car. He wants the house to look
nice. He wants the car to look nice.
What other products could you offer to that guy? If your client is sitting there with a smile
and not promoting it because he doesn’t have more products to sell these people, that

-- 19 of 28 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 5 • Pitching the Deal
Transcript 	Page 20 of 28
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
idea alone can put millions of dollars on his bottom line.
The next part of your proposal should discuss the market and this is where his ideal
prospects are hiding. Look at who his best customers are now in terms of frequency,
average sale, total purchases per year, that kind of thing. Figure out where they came from
in the first place and what the psychographics are.
Psychographics is what are their passions? One of the things I see happening online
right now and this kind of ties into what I just said. If you subscribe to Newsmax, Human
Events, Dredge Report, or any of the other conservative news organizations you’re going
to begin getting a lot of promotions for nutritional supplement products.
If you’re a supplement company and you start looking at the psychographics of your file
and the sources where you got your names and you find out that you’re getting a lot of
names from people who are politically conservative, that gives you a lot of clues of where
to go find a lot more people that are like your best customers now.
In this market segment is where I will tend to outline the lists. This has more to do with
psychography. It has to do with conservatives really like this product or liberals really this
product. Little old ladies who subscribe to knitting magazines really like this product. This
is where you get some clues as to where you should be going next to find another million
or two customers like the 100,000 he may have now.
This is important to do anyway in terms of creating a marketing plan later on. Hopefully
we’ll be able to get to at some point.
Next is the media. You know who you’re looking for, now the question is what’s the most
cost efficient way to get to them? The company you’re working with is going to have one,
possibly two media they’re already using. Chances are they’ll have one that they’re really
doing well, really exploiting fully and understand. Then they’ll have another one or two
they’re dabbling in but there’s huge room for improvement here.
In this section you need to go through and look at all of the available media for that client.
When I say available media, in most cases I’m not talking about, “Well, gee, the local
newspaper is media.”
I’m talking about the psychographically correct media for that client company. On the web,
for example, there are email lists available that you can rent. I hear they don’t always work
that well. Some of them have worked okay for us, but it’s not a prime way to drive traffic.
Affiliate campaigns. Now we’re talking. If you have an investment product you can go to
every other investment guy online who has a list and say, “Let’s all make some money
together. Here’s a product. You sell it to your file.” We’re doing that right now. “Here’s this

-- 20 of 28 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 5 • Pitching the Deal
Transcript 	Page 2 of 28
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
product we’ve created and this whole promotional campaign is already done. Send it to
your file and keep half the money.”
Ezine sponsorships. You pay for the space on somebody else’s ezine. Again, this is an
area if you sell a product to people who like to make model boats you find the ezines that
go to those people and sponsor those ezines that are psychographically correct.
Same thing with banners. You go to sites that make sense. You go to social networking
sites. You work that.
Joint ventures in direct mail. You look at direct mail all the list people who bought products
like yours before.
In print ads you look at all the magazines and newspapers that go to people who are
like the prospects you’re looking for, typically people whose magazines and newspaper
subjects are focused on the thing that you do.
TV infomercials and/or spots. Again the media here that’s available is very broad. If
you’re selling a weight loss product you’ll probably do okay on pretty much any channel.
However, there are also health channels now. So begin with the channels that are most
psychographically correct. Do the same thing with radio infomercials.
Then anything else that makes sense to you based on what the client is selling and who
his prospects are.
The next section is strategy. At this point you’ve identified all kinds of opportunities. You’ve
identified the products you want to sell, the media, who you’re going to sell it to. You’ve
looked at offers and all of these other wonderful things. You have a lot of opportunities
before you.
Now it’s important to list the opportunities that will produce the greatest benefit in the
shortest amount of time at lowest cost. As the rainmaker you want to go in and you want
your first 90 days to be so memorable with that client that he would rather cut off his right
arm than lose you.
The way to do that is find those opportunities where you can produce a huge result quickly
and do those first. List the opportunities in order of how quickly you can get them done and
what the return would be. Include a rough timeline for each project.
Don’t get down to first draft copies due now, second draft here, and final draft here. Legal
compliance will take place on such a date. Just say in the first six weeks we want to do
these three campaigns. Give only rough timelines. Everyone understands that timelines
change.

-- 21 of 28 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 5 • Pitching the Deal
Transcript 	Page 22 of 28
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Now on some of these things you may be a hero and get them done sooner. In some
cases they may get pushed back. Remember that when you’re working with a client there
are an awful lot of people in that mix. When things get held back it’s usually not going to be
your fault.
Include rough costs or cost estimates and a target return for each. This is where you’re
selling the dream, okay? “My idea for you is we’re going to take this product to 1000
affiliates in the first 30 days. They’re going to sign up for a program and together they
have 10 million customers. They’re going to recommend your free report to their 10 million
customers.
“We anticipate that 1-2% of those people are going to download this free report. We also
anticipate 5-6% of those people are actually going to buy your product. That will give you a
revenue of this much, your cost is that much, which means you’re going to clear this much.
The entire campaign will be over and done with by September 30th.”
Now the guy is starting to see the money pile up. He’s starting to see you doing the
work and making it happen. He’s getting excited. You need to do this for the immediate
opportunities that you have.
Relationship -- Specify the work that you’ll do for the client. Cover all the bases. You’re
going to give them marketing and testing strategies, copywriting and graphics direction,
product ideas and development, general consulting.
You’re going to brainstorm with him on all the above. You’re going to give him advice on
his organization, processes, and recruitment resources. You’re not going to let him make
a mistake. You’re going to be there for him. Basically this is where you regurgitate his
answer to, “What do you want me to do for you. What do you want from me? There’s more
to be done here, okay?”
You need to specify how the work will be performed. This is really important. Whenever a
company begins working with an outsider there are a couple of concerns that they have.
The first concern is that you will do or say something that will reflect poorly on the
company. They’re concerned about control. They want to be comfortable that you’re not
going to go out and do a deal, obligate them in some way, or do something that’s going to
embarrass them. They want to feel that they are in control of this process and you’re not a
loose cannon out there somewhere doing who knows what.
I ask for three things. I ask for touch points. These touch points are daily usually with a
client I’m doing a lot of work with. It might be a 7:15 AM phone call. With another client it
may be twice a week phone calls, or just once a week.
This is where we all get together on the phone and we cover what’s going on, here’s

-- 22 of 28 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 5 • Pitching the Deal
Transcript 	Page 2 of 28
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
where we’re at, here’s what we’re dealing with, these are the challenges, here’s how the
schedule looks going in from here, everything’s great.
Control has more to do with reporting and it’s a two-way street. We provide our master
schedule to the client every week. The client provides us with the actual return reports that
they’re receiving from every campaign that is currently running, every single day.
As I’ve said before, I get one every four hours from Weiss. I get a returns report every four
hours that tells me what campaign, how much money came in, how many cancels, how
we’re looking.
Finally, post mortems. This is a big part of control. The client needs to feel comfortable
that when a campaign is done and the dust has settled, every possible lesson that can be
learned from that campaign has been learned. He needs to also be comfortable that those
lessons will be incorporated into all future marketing plans.
I have post mortem meetings after major campaigns. I cover what we did right and what
we did wrong. “Here’s what we need to never do again and here’s what we always need
to do from now on.” This is big money right here and the client needs to feel that’s being
captured and acted on.
Compensation – What you’ll be paid for each type of work. We don’t have time to go into
all of this now. We have gone through it before. I’ll mention some of the different ways that
I’m paid by my various clients.
Advances on projects are 50% when they reserve the time and the second half when the
work is done. This is simply a guarantee against royalties in most cases.
Royalties apply to promotions where large numbers of the promotional will be mailed but
where the clients’ objective is not to make money. Obviously taking a percentage of profits
would be idiotic of me.
For example, a direct mailing for a client. He’s going to a cold list to get new customers.
His goal is to break even on that mailing and get a new customer. So taking a percent
of profits would be stupid. Instead I take a royalty on each piece mailed. It comes to five
cents per piece mailed, $50 per 1,000.
Commissions is when he is looking for a profit. You’re doing a campaign for a client
and he’s looking to make a lot of money on that campaign. A lot of times these are on
fairly small lists, so taking a royalty wouldn’t make sense so you take a percentage of
sales. That can be net sales, gross sales, however it works for you, you just adjust it the
percentage accordingly.

-- 23 of 28 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 5 • Pitching the Deal
Transcript 	Page 2 of 28
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Overrides. If you’re building a big list but you’re not working the back end, somebody else
is, you might ask for a small percentage on sales to customers. “Look, I’m busting my
hump to bring in all these new customers for you and this other guy is over here creaming
the file making all the money. That can’t happen. What I want is an override on your
monthly sales to the customers that I bring in for you.”
These are all deals that I’ve done and have made me a lot of money.
Then there are also flat fees. They are retainers where for say $10,000 a month you’re
guaranteed that you have first shot at my time. It can be whatever amount you want to set.
Finally, stock options are really good for when you’re doing a lot of general consulting, or
partnering with a company that isn’t quantifiable. A company calls you and tells you their
PPC campaigns aren’t working. No problem. You tell them to call this guy or go to this
website and find out what his competitors best search terms are and then call you back.
You can’t charge for that, right? So you deal with that either with a retainer or with a stock
option. Retainer would cover that. Stock options are cool because the client doesn’t have
to pay them now. Essentially in three years you’re going to go to him with your contract
and cash in your options. That can be a huge payday for you.
If you’re good at what you do your client is going to ask you to be exclusive with him at
some point. It’s not a good idea unless there is a very substantial compensation package
for you.
The reason for that is once you do that you will have all your eggs in one basket. If there’s
a tsunami and the company’s wiped out, if the owner gets hit by a bus, a million terrible
things happen and you’re left sitting there. It’s happened to me twice in my career, with no
client but with an overhead. You never want to be in that position.
If a client wants you to be exclusive with them demand some kind of exclusivity
compensation that will cover you if and when that clients business goes away, or you
relationship goes away.
A client sells a business and the new owner no longer wants to work with you. That
happened with me once. I had built the company up and literally quadrupled it. Another
company came in, bought it, and thought they knew marketing better than I did. I quit and
they went bankrupt in six months.
I had a golden parachute in place that if you guys piss me off for any reason at all and I
quit, you have to give me a lot of money, so I was taken care of.
Be sure and specify when you’ll be paid and how you can monitor your compensation. If

-- 24 of 28 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 5 • Pitching the Deal
Transcript 	Page 2 of 28
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
you’re marketing on the web it’s easy. You just get access to their shopping cart. A good
client will give you any reporting you want in this area.
One of the best answers you can give to any objection that you ask for in terms of your
proposal is, “What if you get hit by a bus?”
A company owner will say he’ll never lie to you about your compensation – “What if you
get hit by a bus?
He says he’ll never pay you late – “What if you get hit by a bus?”
He says he’ll always pay you for use of your work product, even if you’re no longer
working with the company – “What if you get hit by a bus?”
It’s a magic phrase, use it.
Make sure you disclose in your proposal that this relationship will be memorialized in a
legal agreement. Everyone’s on board with that. Why? Because you might get hit by a
bus.
Terms of Agreement – I like three years.
Cancellation Clause – I like them to be 90 days and cancellable by either party. That gives
me time to punt if I need to replace a client. In making it clear I might walk with 90 days’
notice, it gives the client pause in terms of some of our dealing.
Then use of your work product after cancellation. “Just because we’re not working
together any doesn’t mean you can use my copy and not pay me a royalty.”
This is the last chapter of my proposal. It’s very short and gives the client a very clear
picture of what will happen next. “We’re going to meet and talk about this proposal, ask
questions and make whatever adjustments are necessary. Once that’s done we’ll send the
proposal to the lawyers and have them draw up the legal agreement.”
I don’t have a lot of time to wrap up, but if you think through this and you’ve done this
the way we’ve talked about here, you’ve already handled every objection, every major
question he could have, and there’s no reason for this not to be a greasy skid straight into
the lawyer’s office.
Go back through it. Think about what we’ve talked about and I’m sure you’ll see that that’s
the case.
Errors to Avoid

-- 25 of 28 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 5 • Pitching the Deal
Transcript 	Page 2 of 28
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
1. 	Lack of preparation. You don’t start with a proposal. The proposal is the middle of
the process. Do your homework.
2. 	Don’t make it a reading assignment. The big mistake especially young writers make
this and also people who know they’re just full of it and are hoping I won’t figure it out – the
major mistake is they feel they need to make it eternal to be immortal.
I don’t need 20 pages to read. I need you to tell me what the vision is, what the challenges
are, how we’re going to overcome those challenges, how much you’re going to charge me.
If you can do that for me in four pages you got me.
If I’ve got to read 30 I’ll put it down next to the john and maybe I’ll look at a few pages the
next time I’m in there. Don’t feel like you have to make it a reading assignment.
3. 	Pompous language and business school jargon. Any contract or proposal that
comes to me saying something like, ”We’re going to facilitate a synergistic relationship,” is
going straight in the trash. It might have been a great idea and made me a billionaire but I
don’t want to work for people that talk like that.
To me it’s a dead giveaway that somebody has read way too many business books and
he’s been reading instead of working and actually producing. Plus, I just don’t like people
like that.
4. 	Excess complexity and unnecessary detail. Give just enough detail so everyone
is clear on what we’re going to do. You can talk about we’re going to test price. Don’t say
we’re going to test price in $39, $49, $59 and $69. Those are matters for meetings later on
into which everyone will have input. All it does is give people an opportunity to object.
5. 	Failing to fully disclose your compensation. We talked about that. Do not be timid.
It’s the old thing, the ugliest guy in bar can get the most beautiful woman if he thinks he
can get the most beautiful woman, if he exudes confidence. The best looking guy in the
bar won’t get her if he’s a timid little mouse.
It’s the same thing here. You present your compensation with a straight face. You tell them
this is what you get, this is what you’re going to charge. I have on many occasions with
clients prior to doing the proposal.
One time, tongue-in-cheek when I was writing a proposal I said, “The purpose of this
proposal is for you to pay me $1 million in the next 12 months. That’s my purpose in this
proposal. How am I going to do that? I’m going to do that by making you $10 million in the
next 12 months and here’s how I’m going to do it.”

-- 26 of 28 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 5 • Pitching the Deal
Transcript 	Page 2 of 28
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Don’t be shy about your fee. It undermines your credibility and it makes you look like a
loser. Let them know right up front you wouldn’t be talking to them if you didn’t believe
they’re going to pay you a whole lot of money in the next 12 months. “I think you’re going
to smile every time you sign a check because you’re going to be making 10 times more
than me.”
6. 	Blowing the deadline. That’s a huge error. It kind of ties into these other things here.
If you put in too much detail – you think it has to sound like something some idiot Harvard
professor wrote – then you’re going to end up blowing a deadline.
The client is going to interpret that as being first of all thinking you’re not that excited about
the project, and secondly, this is someone that blows deadlines that I’m thinking about
partnering with. Get it to them.
7. 	Typos. Make sure you get it proofread. You’re a wordsmith for crying out loud. If
your apostrophes are in the wrong place and you have misspellings and grammatical
errors all the way through, you’re going to have problems.
Those are the fatal errors. We don’t have many questions and we’re out of time so let me
just go back and look real quick.
Cheryl wants to know –
How much detail? Do you just do the bullets or do you get into more details on what you’ll
be doing?
Cheryl, a proposal really is more about the relationship. That’s the thing that is about to get
carved in stone. The how we’re going to get there, the general direction, is also important.
Our general mission, goals and acknowledgement of our challenges, those things are all
very important. Those are things everyone can agree on.
The nitty gritty detail of precisely how we’re going to move forward from here in terms of
products we’re going to introduce first, what the price point is, those kinds of things are
going to doom you. You’re going to end up in long discussions about other ways to do it.
Remember, what you want here is for this to go to an attorney and result in a contract. The
things that are crucial are what kind of work I’m going to do for you and what you’re going
to pay me for it. Everything else you have in the proposal is just a way to get everybody on
the same page.
Any quick copy advice for a left brain thinker?
Well, Cheryl, as a matter of fact, yes. Everyone is going to think we set this one up. Get

-- 27 of 28 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 5 • Pitching the Deal
Transcript 	Page 28 of 28
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Tony’s new product, The Ultimate Desktop Copy Coach. I’ve never seen anything like it.
The thing is phenomenal. I wish I could say I had anything to do with creating this thing. I
didn’t.
Tony went to school on my work and spent time talking to me about it over the last year.
He has actually spent two years studying the successful copy I’ve done online and offline.
He’s got almost 1,100 pages in two huge binders. He’s got six Getting Started audios and
three videos. The thing is amazing.
The real amazing thing of it is that it’s not just a bunch of tips. He goes into the process
I go through and other top copywriters go through to solve problems and write a lead or
fascination. It’s all laid out in such exquisite detail. Plus the way it’s set up.
Let’s say you’re stuck for a headline. You flip over to the page for headlines and problem
solved. You’ve got more ideas than you could shake a stick at.
I strongly suggest you want to look at that, especially since there’s a 100% money back
guarantee on it. You get your money back if you don’t like it.
Alright, guys. I hope this helped. Thanks for attending and I’ll see you next time. Take care,
guys. Bye.

-- 28 of 28 --
